title: Machine Learning
description: Build baseline models by understanding features, training loops, and evaluation.
outcomes: Define features and labels and explain model assumptions in plain language.
Describe the training/validation/testing lifecycle and why splits matter.
Implement a simple regression and classification baseline workflow.
Choose evaluation metrics that match prediction tasks.
Interpret model behavior to reduce overfitting.
Explain how tree-based models fit when patterns are non-linear.
Ship a working project artifact with a clear model rationale.

lessons.title: Features and Labels
lessons.description: Separate what model inputs are and what model output should predict.
checklist: I can define features and labels in plain language.
I can identify target leakage risks in a dataset.
I can suggest one feature engineering improvement.
questions: What is the simplest way to explain a feature vs label?
How can leakage happen before training begins?

lessons.title: Training and Testing
lessons.description: Understand split strategy and evaluation hygiene.
checklist: I can explain why random seeds matter.
I can describe a clean split strategy.
I can avoid a common leakage mistake.
questions: Why can't we evaluate on training data only?
When would stratified splits be important?

lessons.title: Regression
lessons.description: Build first predictive intuition with continuous outcomes.
checklist: I can explain what regression predicts.
I can interpret a slope-like relationship.
I can spot one regression failure mode.
questions: When is regression a good first choice?
What does a high error on validation suggest?

lessons.title: Classification
lessons.description: Predict categories and interpret class-based outcomes.
checklist: I can distinguish regression from classification problems.
I can explain class imbalance consequences.
I can pick a baseline strategy for binary classification.
questions: Why do balanced classes change which metric you choose?
What is a simple baseline for class predictions?

lessons.title: Decision Trees
lessons.description: Learn interpretable branching models and their tradeoffs.
checklist: I can explain how a tree makes decisions.
I can identify signs of depth overuse.
I can compare tree logic with linear models.
questions: Why are trees often easier to explain to non-technical teams?
What happens when a tree grows too deep?

lessons.title: Random Forest
lessons.description: Use many trees together for stronger generalization.
checklist: I can describe the ensemble idea in simple terms.
I can explain variance reduction in a forest.
I can choose when to prefer forest over a single tree.
questions: Why can one forest beat one tree?
How does averaging predictions reduce instability?

lessons.title: Accuracy and Evaluation
lessons.description: Choose and interpret metrics that guide model decisions.
checklist: I can explain accuracy limitations.
I can match a metric to task risk.
I can read a confusion matrix confidently.
questions: Why can accuracy be misleading with imbalance?
What do precision and recall answer for decisions?

lessons.title: Overfitting
lessons.description: Detect and reduce overfitting through validation-aware choices.
checklist: I can describe overfitting with one analogy.
I can detect it in train/val behavior.
I can suggest one mitigation strategy.
questions: Why do good training metrics mislead sometimes?
What is one practical anti-overfitting habit?

lessons.title: Mini Project: Match Score Scoring Check
lessons.description: Prototype a small scoring pipeline with clear train/test separation.
checklist: I can build and document a baseline scorer.
I can track feature and target leakage.
I can compare at least two model variations.
questions: How do you prove your scoreer is not leaking?
How will you communicate uncertainty to users?

lessons.title: Portfolio Project: Job Match Score Predictor
lessons.description: Create a portfolio-ready model that predicts a job fit score from role features.
checklist: I can build a full preprocessing and split pipeline.
I can justify feature choices from business intent.
I can present model limits and next improvements.
questions: What makes your model production-friendly?
Which metric will you trust for this use case and why?

lessons.title: Machine Learning Quiz
lessons.description: Confirm readiness before moving into deep learning topics.
checklist: I can explain feature and target role separation.
I can interpret overfitting and metric fit.
I can defend a basic model choice.
questions: Which part of the model lifecycle most protects product trust?
How do you decide between trees and linear models?

quizQuestions.prompt: In simple terms, what is the difference between a feature and a label?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Features are the inputs used to make a prediction, while the label is the target value the model is trying to predict.

options.label: Model before task
options.explanation: The learning task must be clear before model choice can be meaningful.

options.label: Accuracy for everything
options.explanation: Accuracy can hide poor performance when classes are imbalanced or error costs differ.

options.label: Training-only evaluation
options.explanation: Training performance alone cannot show whether the model generalizes to new examples.

options.label: Inputs versus prediction target
options.explanation: Features are the inputs used to make a prediction, while the label is the target value the model is trying to predict.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why does splitting data correctly matter before model development?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Correct splitting creates an honest evaluation by keeping unseen data separate, so performance reflects behavior in new cases and avoids leakage-driven optimism.

options.label: Accuracy for everything
options.explanation: Accuracy can hide poor performance when classes are imbalanced or error costs differ.

options.label: Training-only evaluation
options.explanation: Training performance alone cannot show whether the model generalizes to new examples.

options.label: Complexity by default
options.explanation: Complexity can increase overfitting and reduce explainability without improving the product outcome.

options.label: Honest unseen-data evaluation
options.explanation: Correct splitting creates an honest evaluation by keeping unseen data separate, so performance reflects behavior in new cases and avoids leakage-driven optimism.

quizQuestions.prompt: When should you use regression instead of classification?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Use regression when outputs are continuous values and classification when outputs are categories or class labels.

options.label: Continuous versus category outputs
options.explanation: Use regression when outputs are continuous values and classification when outputs are categories or class labels.

options.label: Training-only evaluation
options.explanation: Training performance alone cannot show whether the model generalizes to new examples.

options.label: Complexity by default
options.explanation: Complexity can increase overfitting and reduce explainability without improving the product outcome.

options.label: Labels as metadata
options.explanation: Labels define the target a supervised model learns to predict.

quizQuestions.prompt: What makes tree-based models easy to explain to business teams?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Trees split decisions into simple rules, so you can describe prediction steps as conditional questions on features.

options.label: Rule-like decision paths
options.explanation: Trees split decisions into simple rules, so you can describe prediction steps as conditional questions on features.

options.label: Complexity by default
options.explanation: Complexity can increase overfitting and reduce explainability without improving the product outcome.

options.label: Labels as metadata
options.explanation: Labels define the target a supervised model learns to predict.

options.label: Ignore validation trends
options.explanation: Validation and monitoring trends help detect overfitting, drift, and degraded performance.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why does a random forest often generalize better than one decision tree?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: A forest combines many trees on varied samples, and averaging their outputs reduces instability and overfitting compared with a single tree.

options.label: Labels as metadata
options.explanation: Labels define the target a supervised model learns to predict.

options.label: Averaged trees reduce variance
options.explanation: A forest combines many trees on varied samples, and averaging their outputs reduces instability and overfitting compared with a single tree.

options.label: Ignore validation trends
options.explanation: Validation and monitoring trends help detect overfitting, drift, and degraded performance.

options.label: Model before task
options.explanation: The learning task must be clear before model choice can be meaningful.

quizQuestions.prompt: How should metric choice change when classes are imbalanced?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: When imbalance is high, use metrics like precision and recall that reveal minority-class performance, not only overall accuracy.

options.label: Precision and recall for imbalance
options.explanation: When imbalance is high, use metrics like precision and recall that reveal minority-class performance, not only overall accuracy.

options.label: Ignore validation trends
options.explanation: Validation and monitoring trends help detect overfitting, drift, and degraded performance.

options.label: Model before task
options.explanation: The learning task must be clear before model choice can be meaningful.

options.label: Accuracy for everything
options.explanation: Accuracy can hide poor performance when classes are imbalanced or error costs differ.

quizQuestions.prompt: Describe one reliable sign of overfitting in train and validation trends.
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Overfitting is often seen when training metrics improve while validation metrics worsen, suggesting the model has memorized patterns that do not generalize.

options.label: Model before task
options.explanation: The learning task must be clear before model choice can be meaningful.

options.label: Training improves, validation worsens
options.explanation: Overfitting is often seen when training metrics improve while validation metrics worsen, suggesting the model has memorized patterns that do not generalize.

options.label: Accuracy for everything
options.explanation: Accuracy can hide poor performance when classes are imbalanced or error costs differ.

options.label: Training-only evaluation
options.explanation: Training performance alone cannot show whether the model generalizes to new examples.
